AI Weekly Digest #142

March 5, 2026

Welcome to this week's AI roundup. Big moves across the industry as we head into Q2.

Anthropic Launches Claude 5 Family

Anthropic released the Claude 5 model family this week, featuring significant improvements in reasoning, tool use, and long-context performance. The new Opus variant achieves state-of-the-art results on graduate-level science benchmarks while maintaining the safety properties the company is known for. Enterprise customers report 40% faster task completion in early access testing.

Pricing follows a tiered structure: Haiku remains the most affordable option at $0.25/M input tokens, Sonnet targets the mid-range at $3/M, and Opus commands a premium at $15/M for the most demanding workloads.

Open-Source Models Close the Gap

Meta's Llama 4 and Mistral's new Pixtral Large are narrowing the performance gap with proprietary models. Independent evaluations show Llama 4 70B matching GPT-4o on coding benchmarks, while Pixtral Large leads on multilingual tasks. The trend raises strategic questions about the long-term viability of closed-model pricing moats.

EU AI Act Enforcement Begins

The first enforcement actions under the EU AI Act have been announced, targeting three companies for non-compliant high-risk AI systems in recruitment. Fines could reach up to 35 million euros or 7% of global turnover. Legal experts say these early cases will set important precedents for how the regulation is interpreted across member states.

Quick Links

  • Google DeepMind publishes new research on AI-assisted drug discovery (paper)
  • NVIDIA reports record Q4 data-center revenue of $38B (earnings call)
  • Stanford HAI releases 2026 AI Index: global AI investment hits $340B (report)
  • OpenAI launches Operator API for agentic web browsing (docs)
